speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, interview with Vera Ray. You were talking about the anti-Semitism that you experienced in Russia. While you were in Kyzylorda, do you remember other incidents there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. But you see, Kyzylorda is in Kazakhstan, and I don't think they've ever seen a Jew in their lives. They're Asian people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the place that you were accommodated to, were there other refugees together with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I can just remember this little hut, and there were chooks in there, and we were there, and pigs were there. And, and it was very tiny. And there were no windows. And I think I went to school there because I remember a photograph with a lot of children. And I had a Korean boyfriend. Now how did the Korean get to Kazakhstan? I have no idea. But we-- we always played together. His name was Vova. So, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they similar games to what you played in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to look for treasure. We looked for treasure a lot, because I remember, you know, there were all these houses. But that wasn't in Kyzylorda. Forget it. That must have been in Korets because there were all these bombed out houses. See, it gets very messy in my head in those early days, because, I mean, I just have these vignettes. And to actually place them in chronological order is a bit hard. So that's not Korets, no. My main-- my main memories of Korets are these-- these-- these huts and these markets, and basically being surrounded by kids who looked different from us and going to that school and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how they related to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think fine. As I said, I-- I have no memories of being teased or not fitting in. I think I must have just been a novelty. And I was so used to, you know-- I mean, I changed so many schools in so many languages in so many countries before I was 10 years old. It was just another country. I remember my mother said to me that one time we stayed somewhere for three months, and I said, when are we moving again? We were just on the move for, I don't know, five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother doing during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was really big on normalizing things as soon-- and so was my father. As soon as we got somewhere, well, it's like we're staying here forever. I was sent to school straight away, whereas my friend Dorothy had a much freer life. Oh, we'll be moving again. So she was allowed to-- she told me-- run around and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I, straight into school, straight into piano lessons, you know? And my mother, straight into teaching. So, you know, pretending that it's all-- well, no, I'm not going to say pretend-- make it as normal as possible. That was really their way of being, yeah. Where did you practice the piano in-- in Kazakhstan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not in Kyzylorda, but I do remember after Kyzylorda, it was piano practice. Let me tell you. [LAUGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I would say that all these places, under a year because otherwise, they won't all fit in, you see? I mean, there were six years of war, and I was-- I was in, you know, six, seven places at least. So I would say roughly a year, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall-- do you have any memory of where you were or what happened when Russia was invaded by Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been in Korets because there's a song. And I've just played it in my head, which says, July 22 at exactly 4 o'clock, they bombed Kiev, and they told us that the war had started. And that would be about right. So songs are useful sometimes. So we must have been in Korets. And we must have-- that must have been the cattle trucks and the, you know, next journey. So that would have been, what, '42? Boy, I tell you, my chronology is going to be a big problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were only a child, so it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Very difficult --

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --To remember dates.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we must have been in Korets for a couple of years. That's possible. Because, as I said, my mother went and tried to get this training. We would have been in Korets for a couple of years. Yeah, change that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after Kyzylorda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are two or three-- yeah, OK. The next one was Ulyanovsk. Now that was beautiful. It was on the Volga. It's called Ulyanovsk because Lenin was born there, and his-- his original name was Ulyanovsk. And it was on the Volga. And it was the first time that I actually saw ice cream. Amazing strawberry ice cream you could get. And that's where I met-- that's where we ran into, in the street, these people that I was mentioning before, the Altmans. And they told us about a Czech army forming in the depth of Russia, and that they were going there. And so we did, too. I don't remember much about Ulyanovsk. I have really-- it was-- it was almost like the war hadn't touched it. In other words, you could go to a restaurant and actually order, order a meal. Now, there were only two types of meal you could have, [RUSSIAN] or [RUSSIAN]. [RUSSIAN] is hamburgers, and [RUSSIAN] is stew. But you could go and say, I want this, or I want that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to me, that was like-- I hadn't-- you know, I had years of not really knowing how life was. So that was really an amazing break. And I think that's where the piano started-- the piano torment started. There was a lot of music, and I remember a beautiful garden. I don't know where we were staying. That went for a little while. Now, can I ask you, why did you have to leave Kyzylorda.

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't think the Germans got to Asia. Did they get to Asia? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have no idea. I mean, I'm sure we would have wanted to have-- you know, it was really not a sort of place where people would actually-- I mean people who were used to living in a different lifestyle would want to stay. But how we got out of there, and why we got out of there, I just have not got a clue. But I know that that was, again, cattle trucks, and again, miles and miles of train, train stops. And I don't know how we got any food because I remember that all you could get at all those train stations was boiling water. And if you were lucky, you had some sugar. You put it in your mouth, and you drank the boiling water through the sugar. But [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I do remember that we still had a few bits and pieces to sell from the shop. And maybe that's how we did it. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Russian authorities treated you on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: With great suspicion. And they didn't like foreigners. They didn't like Jews. But they needed my father. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My-- it was very-- I've mentioned this before. It was very difficult for my mother to teach because they didn't trust her at all. And discipline in-- in-- in-- in Russia was woeful, woeful. And she had no support. She had to give up teaching in the end because it was just too much. And then she-- but once we got to Jevremov, you see, the Czech army was there, and that was a different story altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Ulyanovsk, you heard about the Czech army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ulyanovsk, I don't know where these Altmans had actually found out about it, but they did. And the Czech army was made up of, I reckon, 90% of Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Absolutely, because I ended up in this home for children. Because in the end, both my parents became soldiers, and, you know, the front started to move backwards, so my parents also did. And they-- they-- they started this home for the soldiers of-- the children of Czech soldiers. And so I ended up there for over 18 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you describe from Ulyanovsk what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Jevremov?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Well, I think it must have been by train because I remember there were-- there were lots and lots of train journeys, lots of train journeys. So and I think we must have gone with-- with the Altmans, but that is also-- yeah, I think so. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Jevremov, how were you accommodated there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jevremov, we lived in a house with a whole lot of people because I remember we slept on this Russian oven. I mean, Russian ovens are the size of a small room, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a lot of bombardment still going. There was a whole lot of stuff going on, you know? It wasn't just like you get on a train and you end up somewhere else. I mean, Stalingrad was being-- that was the battle [INAUDIBLE]. That was a bit later. There was a lot of fear and unrest, and not much food, and all that sort of thing. In this house that we lived, there was an old woman. There was a bombardment, and this old woman lost all her family. And I've got the wording in Yiddish now and in Russian. You think I can think of it in English? The owner, the landlady, took her in out of pity. And-- but the woman was-- you know, she was really quite old and she was incontinent and all this thing. And every morning, the landlady would-- would wake up, and she'd say, [RUSSIAN]. Grandma, are you still alive? And grandma would say, I'm sorry, but I am. So she couldn't wait for her to die to get her out of the house. So we were there, three of us, then the landlady, then the grandmother. Then there was a woman with a small child, because I remember the child screaming a lot, and I had to take it out and-- and quieten it. I was always very good with babies and kids. My daughter takes after me. And so it was pretty crowded. But they built those-- what do you call them where, where, where the soldiers live? What do you call them? [RUSSIAN]. Barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks, thank you. They built those barracks, and then my father had to go and live there. My mother was still allowed to stay. And, and she used to-- she worked-- she was a soldier. She worked in the headquarters with the officers. She was like a secretary there. So she stayed home a bit-- a bit longer. And then the whole caboodle moved from Buzuluk to Jevremov, or the other way around-- or the other way around, Buzuluk to Jevremov, Jevremov to Buzuluk. I think maybe Buzuluk to Jevremov. You know, the whole-- the whole army and the whole thing, we moved there. And after that, they left us there, and they went to the front. And then I didn't see my mother till the end of the war in Czechoslovakia. My father, the good old provider, was so concerned that he somehow wrangled it that he got a job to set up a children's home in Romania because, you know, it looked like the war was going to finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it must have been about 1944, and it looked like the war was going to finish. And so he convinced the authorities that those kids from Russia will have to go somewhere until they're picked up. So he set up this home. And then, he came and got me. He got me, and he got Dorothy, my friend, and two or three other kids, and then the others came later. And so the whole children's home from Buzuluk was moved to, to Bucharest. Before you talk about that, could you describe the way you, you lived when you were away, when your parents were away from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that wasn't all that wonderful. Well, first of all, because I was good with kids, there were two mothers of babies. Like, the babies were what? 12 months, 18 months old. They said to me, Vera, you look after our babies. There was Paula and Tommy. And I took this very seriously. So I used to go downstairs and feed them and delouse them and change their nappies and make sure that they were all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was very-- I felt very responsible. And actually it weighed rather heavily on me, because, you know, it was something I had to do because I promised. And-- Were they Russian women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech. No, no, they were the Czech women who went to the front. That-- this is the Czech-- I'm talking about the Czech home in Russia. And all the parents left their children in this Czech home and went to fight the Germans in-- on the front. The front was moving backwards because the Germans were being defeated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in charge? How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I would have been 10, 11 maybe. I was-- it was quite a [? big ?] responsibility. I felt it as a big responsibility. And, you know, and I was still a naughty girl, and I was still a rebel. And I was very much into reading. I, I read absolutely voraciously. When I think about it now, I really wanted to understand what was happening to me, and nobody was telling me. And somehow or another, I thought if I read a lot, I would understand. I remember being depressed as a child. I think the depression set in when I was about nine or 10. So I read everything. And I remember one time it was compulsory games, and I wanted to read. And the teacher said, no, I couldn't read. I had to go and play. And I didn't want to go, so I said, no, I'm not going. And she told the kids to pick me up bodily and take me to somewhere or another. And, I-- and I just-- I just stood face-to-face to her and said, I am not doing it. You can't make me. And she slapped me really hard across the face. So I slapped her back. Blood down my nose. I was taken to the principal, and that was the night when I wrote to my mother in Russian. And she kept this letter. Mother, if you love me, you will come and get me-- great big tear stains. And 30 years later, my mother said, I'm sick of feeling guilty. Here, take this letter. So I took it, and I burnt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I didn't feel so good, you know? It was all too much, really, by that stage. You know, there were some good times, the kids, you know. I'm a very, sort of, gregarious person, and I liked a lot of people around me. Being an only child, you couldn't-- you know, it wasn't possible. So there were some times when I was-- but we were all very-- we were very unhappy, really. I mean, you know, we were only little, basically.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I know kids nowadays go to-- to boarding school, but they have holidays. They can go home. They can ring up mom. And we were just like, this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So any of the adults in the home gave you any support with what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. The poor old teacher who hit me, she really meant well by me. She want-- she didn't want me to sit and mope over a book. She wanted me to go and play ball. But I was reading Byron. And I was in the Byronic year, you know Byron, and Pushkin, and Lermontov, and all these. And I remember cutting a piece of hair off and saying, I'm going to be a rebel. I needed a personality, an identity. I didn't know what-- you know, I didn't know who I was. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you read in?

speaker: interviewee
text: --she meant well. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you read these books in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russian by that stage. So it wasn't all black, but deep down, it was black, you know? You had fun. You were naughty. You were good. You did this. You did that. But you were alone. That was my main feeling, that feeling of, I am here alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that stage, did you have any knowledge or did you hear adults talking about what was happening with the Jews in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure did. [DRINKS] Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard about that the Germans were killing the Jews, and that my father was very smart to, to have done this. And my mother, you know, she-- she really appreciated some of the stuff that my father did to get us out because we knew that that train, and there's women here and children there, that was all baloney, that they all ended up in concentration camps. And we knew. I mean, we knew why we were running. We-- we knew what was going on before we left. That's why we left. Of course we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get these messages then in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, you see, we had a lot of-- you know, the Czech army in Russia was actually financed by America and England because the government of Czechoslovakia-- Benes and Masaryk, and all these people-- they were in England. And UNRRA-- U, N, double R, A-- I don't know what that stands for-- United Nations Refugees Appeal, I don't know-- they were-- they were sending us parcels and things. I remember wearing these weird clothes that the kids laughed at because they were American, overalls, you know? It was sort of second hand stuff that they were sending us. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the information would have, I presume, come from out there because there was-- because the whole army-- I mean, we wore English uniforms. You will see it on the-- on the photos. They were not Russian uniforms. How was it for you to hear things like that as a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was just-- it was like-- it was a futility. [INAUDIBLE] Just a phrase came to me in Russian. [RUSSIAN]. There's nothing you can do about it. That's the war. It was a helplessness. It was a shrugging of the shoulders. Yeah, it wasn't very healthy. It was-- it was giving in, but what could you do? That's how it felt like. What can you do? That's how it felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about things like that with other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: With other children, no. I did talk to, when I came back to Prague, I spoke to a girl who, who just came out of Terezin, and she now lives in-- in Israel. And we-- you know, that was the first time that we actually talked. But by that stage, I was 12, 12 and 13. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't talk. You know, we accepted that life is awful. You just go, yeah, life is awful. So what else is new? And being in Russia and being, you know, really not wanted there, they didn't want us there. Was it there that you experienced the anti-Semitism that you described before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was either in Jevremov or Buzuluk. I get-- those two places are so mixed up, because that was one after the other, and it was with the-- with the army. It was once I was in the army. And that constant denial of being Jewish, this is what really annoys me now, more than anything. However, as I have been told by, by a number of therapists, that was the way you saved yourself. Thank your mother that she-- that she taught you to deny. All right, thanks, mum. I wish it was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what were the-- what was the Russian authority's attitude to the Czech army in Soviet Union?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I think it was relatively friendly. I'm just thinking whether they-- I'm trying to think what my dad used to say, whether he-- whether he said that they pushed them in the front in front of them. I don't think so. I think they saw them as, you know, the Slavonic brotherhood or something. But I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying before that you sent a letter to your mother. Did she reply?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just amazed she got the letter. Did she reply? I don't think so. Well, I can't remember getting a reply. So, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Buzuluk or Jevremov? Well, that's when my dad came and took us to, to Romania. Another train trip through Moscow, but boy that was much better because I had my father. That was much better.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he come to organize this home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I honestly don't know. Because he was with-- because he was in charge of food, or something to do with the officer's mess, or I don't know what, he must have told them that, look, I know how to look after kids. I've got a kid. I know how to look after food. I can organize a house. Let me do it. And I'm sure nobody else had a-- had the idea, because he was an ideas man, you know? He probably just thought it up. It was a nice house. I don't know how many kids ended up there in the end. Would have been at least 20, 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they choose Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Romania was the closest-- it was the closest to-- but the Germans weren't there anymore, and the Russians weren't there. You see, it was getting us out of Russia. That wasn't the easiest thing on earth because I remember going to Romanian school. I mean, I have been to so many schools of language. I really have to laugh when people worry about their kids changing schools. For heaven's sakes, kids are much tougher than we think they are. OK, so Romania, I went to Romanian school. But it was fun. I started to enjoy myself. And I started to be naughty again, so life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now we'll finish the tape.
